content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES Co1IMIssIoN
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
• Sacramento. CA 958 12-0807
(916) 322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
July 7, 1998
Mark D. Hensley
Burke, Williams & Sorensen, LLP
611 West Sixth Street, Suite 2500
Los Angeles, California 90017-3 102
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-98-160
Dear Mr. Hensley:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Mike Gordon, Nancy
Wemick, and John Gaines regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTIONS
1. May Councilmember Nancy Wemick participate in decisions regarding a land use
project involving Federal Express?
•
2. May Mayor Mike Gordon participate in decisions regarding a land use project
involving Federal Express?
3. May Councilmember John Gaines participate in decisions regarding a land use project
involving Federal Express?
4. How does the rule of necessity affect each ofthe public official’s ability to participate
in a land use project involving Federal Express?
CONCLUSION
1. No. Councilmember Wernick may not participate in the decision since it will have a
reasonably foreseeable and material fmancial effect on Federal Express. See Analysis below.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-98-160
Page 2
2. Mayor Gordon may not participate in the decision if it will have a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect on Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc., or on his interest in
real property located approximately 700 feet from the project. See Analysis below.
3. Councilmember Gaines may not participate in the decision if it will have a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect on Lockheed Martin or, if Lockheed Martin merges with
the Northrup Grumann Corporation, a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on the
company created by the merger. See Analysis below.
4. If all three public officials are disqualified pursuant to the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act, then each public official may participate in a random, impartial, and
equitable process to choose which one of the three may participate in the decision in order to
create a quorum. See Analysis below.
FACTS
Federal Express has submitted an application to the City ofEl Segundo (the “city”) for a
land use project (“project”) determination which could ultimately be presented to the city council
for review and action. The city has five councilmembers and three councilmembers constitute a
quorum of the council. The mayor and two councilmembers have possible conflicts of interest
relating to the project.
Councilmember Nancy Wernick’s spouse is currently an employee of Federal Express
and she has received in excess of $250 in income from Federal Express in the preceding twelve
months.
Mayor Mike Gordon is an officer and employee, as well as a fifly percent shareholder, of
Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc., a corporation which leases office space in a building which is
located within approximately 700 feet ofthe project. The lease expires on June 30, 2000. Mayor
Gordon believes it is possible he will seek to renegotiate the lease when it expires.
Councilmember John Gaines is currently an employee of Lockheed Martin which is
attempting to merge with Northrup Grumann Corporation. The Northrup Grumann Corporation
owns property which is located within approximately 150 feet of the project. The merger has
been challenged by the United State Department of Justice. Newspaper reports declare the deal
is all but dead. However, Lockheed Martin is continuing to attempt to complete the merger.


File No. A-98-160
Page 3
ANALYSIS
I. Introduction
The Act was adopted by the people ofthe State of California by initiative in 1974. The
purpose of the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to insure that public officials perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests ofpersons who support them. (Section 8 1001(b).) In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official knows
or has reason to know he or she has a financial interest.
II. Financial Interests
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally,
2 on the official or a member of his or her immediate
family or on any of the following economic interests:
“(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct
or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
more.
(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or
indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a
gift or gifts aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more
in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. The
2 It does not appear that the public generally rule applies to your facts and, therefore, will not be discussed
here. Please see Regulation 18703.
The gift limit for 1997 and 1998 is $290. (Regulation 18940.2.)


File No. A-98-160
Page 4
amount of the value of gifts specified by this subdivision shall be
adjusted biennially by the commission to equal the same amount
determined by the commission pursuant to subdivision (f) of
Section 89503.”
Regulation 18702.1 (a)(4) identifies the final type of economic interest under Section
87103. Specifically, a public official has a financial interest under Regulation 18702.1 (a)(4) if
the governmental decision will have a “personal effect” on him or her or his or her immediate
family,
4 whether positive or negative, of at least $250 in any 12-month period. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).)
Regulation 18702.1(a)(4) does not apply to a financial effect on the
value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the official, or a financial effect on the
gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the
official has an investment interest.
ifi. Councilmember Wernick
A.
Economic Interest #1
- Federal Express
Income includes the community property interest in the income of a spouse. (Section
82030.) If Councilmember Wernick’s spouse has received $500 or more during the past twelve
months from Federal Express, then the councilmember’s community property interest is $250 or
more. Ifthat is the case, Councilmember Wernick has an economic interest in Federal Express
and may not participate in a governmental decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material fmancial effect on Federal Express. (Section 87103(c).)
1. Foreseeability
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a
governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable ifthere is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty
is not required. However, if an effect is a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In
re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Any financial effect, no matter how small, will result in
the standard being met as long as there is a substantial likelthood that the effect will occur.
Federal Express’ land use project will certainly have a financial effect on Federal Express.
Therefore, the foreseeability element is met.
“Immediate family’ means the spouse and dependent children.” (Section 82029.)


File No. A-98-160
Page 5
2. Materiality
Disqualification is still only required where the foreseeable effect on the public official’s
economic interest is material. The Commission has adopted a series of regulations that provide
guidance concerning whether the foreseeable financial effects of a decision are material.
(Regulation 18702.) The standard of materiality differs depending on the type of economic
interest involved and whether the economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in a
decision. (See Regulation 18702.1.)
Since Federal Express submitted an application to the city regarding the land use project,
Federal Express is directly involved. Regulation 18702.1(a) provides that the materiality
standard is met if a source of income is directly involved in the governmental decision.
Therefore, Councilmember Wernick may not participate in a decision involving the land use
project.
IV.
Mayor Gordon
A.
Economic Interest #1
- Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc.
Since Mayor Gordon presumably has an investment in Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc., of
$1,000 or more, receives income of $250 or more from Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc., and
because he is an officer of Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc., Mayor Gordon has an economic
interest in Gordon & Schwenkrneyer, Inc. Therefore, he may not participate in a governmental
decision that has a teasonably foreseable and material financial effect on Gordon &
Schwenkmeyer, Inc.
1. Foreseeability
The foreseeability standard is the same as above. However, we cannot determine whether
the project will have a reasonably foreseeable effect on Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc. No
information has been provided regarding the project or how it may affect Gordon &
Schwenlcmeyer, Inc. The Commission is not the finder of fact when providing advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Therefore, you must determine whether or not there will be a
reasonably foreseeable effect on Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc.
2. Materiality
Assuming foreseeability, disqualification is only required where the reasonably
foreseeable effect is material. Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc., is indirectly involved in the
governmental decision at issue. (Regulation 18702.1(b).) Therefore, Regulation 18702.2
provides the applicable materiality standard. The precise standard depends on the size of


File No. A-98-160
Page 6
Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc. (See Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed).) Like foreseeability,
you must determine whether the materiality standard is met.
B.
Economic Interest #2
- Leasehold Interest in Office Space
Section 82033 states, “Interests in real property of an individual include a pro rata share
of interest in real property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate
family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater.” Therefore,
Mayor Gordon has an interest in any real property held by Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc., worth
$2,000 or more.
Section 82033 and Regulation 18233 together provide that an “interest in real property”
includes any leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest or an option to acquire such an interest in
real property located in the jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public
official unless the interest is a periodic tenancy of one month or less. Therefore, Mayor Gordon
has an economic interest in the real property located approximately 700 feet from the project.
1. Foreseeability
The foreseeability standard is the same as above. Again, we do not have sufficient facts
to determine whether this standard is met.
2. Materiality
Assuming foreseeability, disqualification is only required if the reasonably foreseeable
financial effect is material. For a leasehold interest indirectly involved in a decision, Regulation
18702.4 provides the applicable standard ofmateriality:
“The effect of a decision is material as to a leasehold interest in
real property if any of the following applies:
(a) The decision will change the legally allowable use of the
leased property, and the lessee has the right to sublease the
property;
(b) It is reasonably foreseeable that the lessee will change the
actual use of the property as a result of the decision;
(c) It is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will result in a
change in the actual use of property within 300 feet of the leased
property, and the changed use will significantly enhance or
significantly decrease the use or enjoyment ofthe leased property;
(d) The decision will increase or decrease the amount ofrent for
the leased property by $250 or 5 percent, whichever is greater,
during any 12-month period following the decision; or


File No. A-98-160
Page 7
(e) The decision will result in a change in the termination date of
the lease.”
You will have to apply the above regulation to the facts to determine whether the
materiality standard is met as to this economic interest. If the materiality standard is met, then
Mayor Gordon may not participate in any decision involving the project.
V.
Councilmember Gaines
A.
Financial Interest #1
- Lockheed Martin is a Source ofIncome.
Councilmember Gaines has an economic interest in Lockheed Martin since presumably it
is a source of income to Councilmember Gaines of $250 or more during the previous twelve
months. Therefore, he may not participate in a governmental decision that will have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on Lockheed Martin. (See “IV. A.” for the
applicable foreseeability and materiality standards.) Ifthe Nothrup Grumann Corporation
merges with Lockheed Martin prior to the governmental decision, then Councilmember Gaines
may not participate in any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial
effect on the new business entity that will be created when the two companies merge. (Again,
see “IV. A.” for the applicable standards.)
B.
Financial Interest #2 - Property Owned by Northrup Grumann
Since Counbilmember Gaines does not have an ownership interest in Lockheed Martin or
the Northrup Grumann Corporation, he does not have an economic interest in the real property
owned by the Northrup Grumann Corporation that is located within 150 feet of the project.
VI. Legally Required Participation
If an official is disqualified from making a decision because he or she has a conflict of
interest under the Act, the official may still participate in the decision under the rule of legally
required participation. (Section 87101.) Regulation 18701 interprets and implements the rule of
legally required participation (copy enclosed).
The City Council of El Segundo has five city councilmembers. Three councilmembers
constitute a quorum. If Mayor Gordon and Councilmembers Wernick and Gaines have a conflict
of interest under the Act, then the rule of legally required participation does apply to your facts
since there would only be two councilmembers left and three are need to constitute a quorum.
Accordingly, all three public officials would participate in a random, impartial, and equitable
selection process to choose which one official can participate in the decision in order to create a
quorum. (In re Hudson (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 13.)


File No. A-98-160
Page 8
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Marte Castanos
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures

in a land use project involving Federal Express?

(d) Any business entity in which the public official is director,

amount of the value of gifts specified by this subdivision shall be

IV.
Mayor Gordon
A.
Economic Interest #1
- Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc.
Since Mayor Gordon presumably has an investment in Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc., of
$1,000 or more, receives income of $250 or more from Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc., and
because he is an officer of Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc., Mayor Gordon has an economic
interest in Gordon & Schwenkrneyer, Inc. Therefore, he may not participate in a governmental

Schwenkmeyer, Inc.
1. Foreseeability
The foreseeability standard is the same as above. However, we cannot determine whether
the project will have a reasonably foreseeable effect on Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc. No
information has been provided regarding the project or how it may affect Gordon &
Schwenlcmeyer, Inc. The Commission is not the finder of fact when providing advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Therefore, you must determine whether or not there will be a
reasonably foreseeable effect on Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc.
2. Materiality
Assuming foreseeability, disqualification is only required where the reasonably
foreseeable effect is material. Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc., is indirectly involved in the
governmental decision at issue. (Regulation 18702.1(b).) Therefore, Regulation 18702.2
provides the applicable materiality standard. The precise standard depends on the size of
Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc. (See Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed).) Like foreseeability,
you must determine whether the materiality standard is met.
B.
Economic Interest #2
- Leasehold Interest in Office Space
Section 82033 states, “Interests in real property of an individual include a pro rata share
of interest in real property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate
family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater.” Therefore,

to determine whether this standard is met.
2. Materiality
Assuming foreseeability, disqualification is only required if the reasonably foreseeable
financial effect is material. For a leasehold interest indirectly involved in a decision, Regulation
18702.4 provides the applicable standard ofmateriality:
“The effect of a decision is material as to a leasehold interest in
real property if any of the following applies:

leased property, and the lessee has the right to sublease the
property;
(b) It is reasonably foreseeable that the lessee will change the
actual use of the property as a result of the decision;
(c) It is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will result in a
change in the actual use of property within 300 feet of the leased
property, and the changed use will significantly enhance or
significantly decrease the use or enjoyment ofthe leased property;
(d) The decision will increase or decrease the amount ofrent for
the leased property by $250 or 5 percent, whichever is greater,
during any 12-month period following the decision; or
(e) The decision will result in a change in the termination date of
the lease.”
You will have to apply the above regulation to the facts to determine whether the
materiality standard is met as to this economic interest. If the materiality standard is met, then
Mayor Gordon may not participate in any decision involving the project.
V.
Councilmember Gaines
A.
Financial Interest #1
- Lockheed Martin is a Source ofIncome.
Councilmember Gaines has an economic interest in Lockheed Martin since presumably it
is a source of income to Councilmember Gaines of $250 or more during the previous twelve
months. Therefore, he may not participate in a governmental decision that will have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on Lockheed Martin. (See “IV. A.” for the
applicable foreseeability and materiality standards.) Ifthe Nothrup Grumann Corporation
merges with Lockheed Martin prior to the governmental decision, then Councilmember Gaines
may not participate in any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial
effect on the new business entity that will be created when the two companies merge. (Again,
see “IV. A.” for the applicable standards.)
B.
Financial Interest #2 - Property Owned by Northrup Grumann
Since Counbilmember Gaines does not have an ownership interest in Lockheed Martin or
the Northrup Grumann Corporation, he does not have an economic interest in the real property
owned by the Northrup Grumann Corporation that is located within 150 feet of the project.
VI. Legally Required Participation
If an official is disqualified from making a decision because he or she has a conflict of
interest under the Act, the official may still participate in the decision under the rule of legally
required participation. (Section 87101.) Regulation 18701 interprets and implements the rule of
legally required participation (copy enclosed).
The City Council of El Segundo has five city councilmembers. Three councilmembers

since there would only be two councilmembers left and three are need to constitute a quorum.

QUESTION: 1. May Councilmember Nancy Wemick participate in decisions regarding a land use
project involving Federal Express?
•
2. May Mayor Mike Gordon participate in decisions regarding a land use project
involving Federal Express?
3. May Councilmember John Gaines participate in decisions regarding a land use project
involving Federal Express?
4. How does the rule of necessity affect each ofthe public official’s ability to participate
in a land use project involving Federal Express?

CONCLUSION: 1. No. Councilmember Wernick may not participate in the decision since it will have a
reasonably foreseeable and material fmancial effect on Federal Express. See Analysis below.Page 2
2. Mayor Gordon may not participate in the decision if it will have a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect on Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc., or on his interest in
real property located approximately 700 feet from the project. See Analysis below.
3. Councilmember Gaines may not participate in the decision if it will have a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect on Lockheed Martin or, if Lockheed Martin merges with
the Northrup Grumann Corporation, a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on the
company created by the merger. See Analysis below.
4. If all three public officials are disqualified pursuant to the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act, then each public official may participate in a random, impartial, and
equitable process to choose which one of the three may participate in the decision in order to
create a quorum. See Analysis below.

See Analysis below. 4. If all three public officials are disqualified pursuant to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, then each public official may participate in a random, impartial, and equitable process to choose which one of the three may participate in the decision in order to create a quorum. See Analysis below. FACTS Federal Express has submitted an application to the City ofEl Segundo (the “city”) for a land use project (“project”) determination which could ultimately be presented to the city council for review and action. The city has five councilmembers and three councilmembers constitute a quorum of the council. The mayor and two councilmembers have possible conflicts of interest relating to the project. Councilmember Nancy Wernick’s spouse is currently an employee of Federal Express and she has received in excess of $250 in income from Federal Express in the preceding twelve months. Mayor Mike Gordon is an officer and employee, as well as a fifly percent shareholder, of Gordon & Schwenkmeyer, Inc., a corporation which leases office space in a building which is located within approximately 700 feet ofthe project.
